title: Philosophy & Literature - Iris Murdoch & Bryan Magee (1977)
text:
Some of the great philosophers have also been 
great writers in the sense of literary artists.  
I suppose the outstanding examples are Plato, 
St. Augustine, Schopenhauer, and Nietzsche.  
And if not quite in their 
class, Descartes, Pascal,  
Berkeley, Hume, Rousseau, were certainly very 
good writers anyway. And in our own time,  
Bertrand Russell and Jean-Paul Sartre have both 
been awarded the Nobel Prize for literature.
But on the other hand, some great philosophers 
have been bad writers. Two of the very greatest,  
Kant and Aristotle, being two of the 
worst. Quite a few are just pedestrian.  
One thinks, for instance, of Aquinas or Locke.
As for Hegel, his work has become a byword 
for obscurity, almost a joke in this respect.  
He must be the most difficult to 
read of all world famous writers.
What these examples show 
is that philosophy as such  
is not a branch of literature. If a philosopher 
writes well, it's little more than a bonus. It  
makes him more enticing to study obviously. But 
it does nothing to make him a better philosopher.  
A philosopher can be in the very highest rank 
of all, like Kant, and still be a lousy writer.
I state this firmly at the outset because in 
this program we are going to consider some  
of the areas where philosophy and literature do 
overlap, not only in the work of philosophers who  
happen to be great writers, but also in the work 
of other kinds of writers -- poets, playwrights,  
novelists -- who have been directly 
influenced by philosophical ideas.  
We have someone here whose experience spans both 
these worlds. Iris Murdoch is now a novelist  
of international reputation. But for many years 
before she became a successful novelist -- and,  
indeed, for some years after -- she was a 
tutor in philosophy at Oxford University.
Iris Murdoch, when you're writing a novel 
on the one hand and philosophy on the other,  
are you actually conscious 
that you're engaged in two  
radically different kinds of writing? Are you 
conscious that you're writing differently?
Oh, very much so, yes. These two branches 
of thought have such different aims and such  
different styles. And I feel very strongly that 
one should keep them apart from each other. That  
is, when one's writing philosophy, one's writing 
something which is very much more like science in  
a way than it's like literature. And literature, 
of course, has its own rules and its own tricks.  
And philosophy aims to clarify. And, in fact, 
it's essential to philosophy that should be in  
some sense clarification. And literature is very 
often mystification. And, besides, literature is  
for fun. Literature entertains. Literature does 
many, many things. And philosophy does one thing.
It seems to me -- having read many of your books,  
including your philosophical books -- that the 
actual sentences themselves are different. I mean,  
what strikes me when I read one 
of your novels, the sentences are,  
so to speak, opaque in the sense that there's a 
lot in them. There's a heavy cargo. There's lots  
of connotation and allusion and ambiguity and 
so on, whereas in your philosophical writing,  
the sentences are translucent. They're 
only saying one thing at a time.
Yes. Well, I hope this is so. Obviously, one 
has a style as a writer. And I certainly have  
a probably recognizable style as a novelist. But 
my philosophical style would be something totally  
different. And I would be very pleased if it was 
impossible to tell it was the same person writing.
You said something a moment ago that interested 
me very much. You said that philosophy -- I think  
I've remembered your words correctly. You said 
the aim of philosophy is to clarify. And the aim  
of literature is to mystify. And I suppose it's 
central to what the novelist or the playwright  
is doing, that he's actually trying to create 
an allusion, whereas it's central to what the  
philosopher is doing that he's trying to dispel 
allusion, or pierce through allusion or free--  
liberate his and our minds from allusion. 
Is that an essential part of the difference?
Yes, certainly. And philosophy is to 
do with getting hold of a problem and  
holding on to it relentlessly and doggedly. 
And this is the mark of a philosophical mind,  
this particular relentlessness, the 
fact that you stick to your problem.
With the making of the contrast in mind, 
let's deal with these one at a time. I mean,  
how would you characterize literature as 
distinct from philosophy in this context?
Well, I don't think one can offer exactly a 
definition of literature nor is it necessary.
Some of the characteristics.
We know what literature is. It's an 
art form using words. And, I would say,  
that literature is art by definition. I mean, 
one could define it by contrasting it with,  
say, journalism or something. I dare say 
that great journalism is literature. But  
there are all sorts of borderlines here. 
And literature is very various. And one  
thing that strikes one, of course, 
is that philosophy is very tiny.
If you think how small European philosophy is...
Just a small body of work.
Yes, I mean, it's not a vast and multifarious 
area. What strikes one very much, I think, is  
the way in which the problems, which were 
stated at the beginning, are still there,  
which some people might have forgotten is only 
proof that the world is not making much progress.
The philosopher Whitehead once characterized 
this rather strikingly, didn't he, by saying  
that all Western philosophy is merely footnotes 
to Plato. And there's a sense in which that is  
true. I mean, Plato mapped out the territory, 
and we've all been working in it ever since.
I think the more one studies Plato, the 
more that one sees that this is true,  
that he raised practically every problem 
and raised it in a very sophisticated way.
Well, literature is vast and multifarious 
and multi form. It's not, of course,  
entirely to do with fictions. I mean, there 
are kinds of literature which aren't fiction.
But -- Historical writing and essays 
-- Reflections of all kinds, but  
typical literature -- stories and 
poems and so on -- is fictional.
It's used to do partly with playing roles. This 
is true to some extent of poetry and, I suppose,  
almost entirely, perhaps, one should say true 
of the novel. It's to do with masks and roles  
and pretending and imagining in that sense 
and storytelling. And in this sense, I think,  
literature is very natural. It's 
very close to our own real life.
We all tell stories, you mean?
We all tell stories, yes.
This is the most natural thing 
in the world, telling stories.
I mean, one is doing it all the 
time, possibly without noticing it.
If one describes one's day, as it were, one 
is shaping one's material into a story form.
Very often, it's a funny story incident.
Don't you think it's a very important fact?
There aren't many jokes in philosophy.
No, there are witticisms but not exactly 
jokes. I think jokes would be out of place.  
Somehow we live in a literary atmosphere.
I mean, we live and breathe literature to 
some extent in that when we tell stories or  
when we write letters and so on, we are making 
a form out of something which might be formless.
This is one, of course, of the deep motives for  
literature or for art of any sort that one 
is defeating the formlessness of the world.
One is imposing order on one's experience 
-- Yes, one is cheering one's self up.
And consoling one's self and also, 
of course, instructing one's self by  
giving a form to something, which is perhaps 
alarmingly formless in its original condition.  
A sort of rubble, as if we lived in a kind of 
rubble world, and one's always making forms.
Yes, when you say that one's cheering one's 
self up, I mean, that brings to the floor  
the fact that one of the essential aims of 
literature has always been to entertain,  
hasn't it. And I don't think that has anything 
whatever to do with the motive of philosophy.
No. I think it may be that philosophy 
also cheers us up, of course,  
because philosophy is the imposition of form on 
the formless in a vast way. But I think this is  
something which -- insofar as the 
philosopher might be aware of this,  
it's something that he would be suspicious of 
in himself because one's temptation to produce  
a hasty form or an inappropriate 
form in order to console one's self.
This temptation is one that the philosopher, 
of course, has to avoid. And I think the good  
philosopher is always on doing his own work. 
This is part of the holding onto the problem,  
being dogged and relentless, which would also, 
of course, militate against certain kinds of  
literary devices because it means that one 
has to repeat one's self, that philosophy  
is repetitive. It's coming back over the same 
ground and breaking the form that you've made.
You've made a number of points here, especially 
in your characterization of literature,  
which by implication contrasts with philosophy. 
But I'd like now to, as it were, draw out the  
contrasts explicitly.
For example, you said just now that -- 
and I think it's a striking fact about  
storytelling -- you said that 
it's natural and that we all do  
it. And it's part of everyday life. And, 
certainly, we all like being told stories,  
whereas I suppose, by contrast, philosophy 
is, as it were, counter natural.
I mean, philosophy involves, essentially,  
the critical analysis of one's beliefs 
and the presuppositions of one's beliefs.
And it's very striking that people actually don't  
like having that done to them. None of 
us do. We don't like being analyzed.
And we don't like the assumptions on which our 
beliefs rest being questioned because it makes  
us feel insecure and uncomfortable. And we 
all have a strong resistance to doing it.
Yes, I think it is a very counter natural, a 
very unnatural activity. And when one starts  
doing philosophy -- I mean, I notice this, of 
course, in teaching philosophy that people are  
very reluctant to play this game or cannot see how 
it's done at all to begin with because it involves  
a very odd sort of standpoint.
It's notoriously difficult to define philosophy, 
of course, to somebody that doesn't know  
what this activity is. It's very difficult 
to say what it is. It's to do with conceptual  
structures. It's to do with very deep structures 
of belief and knowledge. It's to do with  
meaning, significance, partly, of course, to 
do with words in the sense that it's about  
how language relates to the 
world and that sort of thing.
And it's not science. And it's not art. And 
it's very important that it's not science I  
think. Don't you think? I mean, as soon as you 
start doing science, you're falling right out of  
philosophy. It's a kind of point of view which, 
although the style may be scientific, the actual  
what you're doing is certainly not science. 
It's a kind of a reflection on concepts.
Yes. Well, Bertrand Russell once said that it's 
questions that we don't know how to answer.  
And I think Isaiah Berlin and other well-known 
contemporary philosophers also take this view.
And it means looking at things which one takes for  
granted and suddenly seeing 
that they're very odd indeed.
But the one thing that philosophy does have 
in common with science, I think, is that it is  
based on an attempt to understand the world and to 
understand our environment and to understand our  
situation in a way that doesn't consist just of 
expressing personal attitudes and personal views.
There is an attempt to, as it were, to submit 
one's self-- and this was implicit in something  
you said just now, I think-- to submit 
one's self to criteria outside one's self.
In other words, you're trying to say something 
that is impersonally true. And you said just now,  
when you were talking about the way you 
yourself write, you said -- and it's absolutely  
true -- that your novel writing has a very 
distinctive literary personality, whereas, I think  
you said, you wouldn't mind if your philosophical 
writing was thought not to have any personality.
Now, it strikes me that almost 
the most important thing about  
an imaginative or creative writer is this 
possession of a literary personality. If he  
hasn't got a distinctive personality in that 
sense, we're not interested in reading it.
Whereas with philosophers, it just isn't the 
case. I mean, you could read all the works of Kant  
with great care and interest and at the end of it 
have no idea what Kant was like as a human being.
Yes. Of course, literature is various in this 
respect, isn't it, that some people have a  
distinctive style. Other people don't. I suppose 
it is self expression, in a way, by definition.
I mean, unless you wanted  
to express yourself -- I mean, if you'd 
didn't have this motive -- you'd lack a very  
important motive towards this form of art, whereas 
philosophy is certainly not self expression.
And philosophy of course is argument. And  
you can say, well, is the conclusion 
true? Or is the argument valid?
I've found when talking to friends of mine 
-- who may be very intelligent and very well  
educated but don't know much about philosophy 
-- I find they often express the assumption  
that philosophy is a branch of literature, that 
a philosopher is somehow expressing a personal  
view of the world in the way that a 
poet might be or a novelist might be.
And it's not always easy to 
explain briefly why this is not so.  
I suppose it's partly because these problems, 
philosophical problems have histories.  
And each individual philosopher comes on the scene 
at some stage in that developing history. And  
if he makes a contribution at all, it's rather 
like the scientist, he makes it at that stage.  
Otherwise, there isn't one to be made so to speak.
Yes, philosophy is the history of philosophy 
in the sense that there is a development in the  
problems. And philosophy lives on philosophy 
very much more than art lives on art.
Yes.
And I think the writing of art -- I mean the 
writing of novels and poems -- engages very  
much more of the personality of both the writer 
and the reader actually than philosophy does.
Philosophy is a much more 
narrowly intellectual activity.
In fact, I'd go farther.
I'd say that literature, to be literature 
at all, must move one emotionally.
Whereas the philosopher, like the 
scientist, is actually trying to exclude  
emotional appeal from his work.
Yes. I mean, literature could be 
said to be a sort of discipline  
technique for arousing certain emotions.
That's certainly one of the reasons why one 
enjoys it. And one of the reasons, perhaps, why  
it's both good for us when it's 
good and bad for us when it's bad.
Well, we've been talking about the differences. 
And I think it's very important that we should  
stress these differences. But there are also 
some things in common, aren't there? I mean,  
I know from previous conversations with you, for 
example, that you think that some notion of truth,  
though they may be different notions of 
truth, are very near the center of both.
Yes. I certainly hold a particular view on the 
subject of what art is like and what literature  
is like. And this view, which wouldn't 
be shared by everybody, by any means,  
would assimilate philosophy and 
literature just in that way. That is,  
I think this may be perhaps the only 
important thing they have in common.
What is important is that they're 
both connected with truth,  
that they are truth-seeking, 
truth-revealing activities in some sense.
And they're both -- I mean, it's 
always illuminating in a particular  
thought form to say, well how is it criticized,  
what kind of critical vocabulary is 
directed against it. And I think that  
the question of truthfulness comes into criticism 
of literature as well as criticism of philosophy.
Could you say a little more about this because 
I think this is particularly interesting.
Well, if one thinks of the kinds 
of things that we say about  
literature from a critical point of view, we often 
condemn it for being sentimental or for being  
fantastic in a bad sense.
I mean, let's establish, say, a contrast between 
imagination and fantasy, with imagination being  
thought of as a good creative power and fantasy 
as a sort of a private self consolation.
So that fantasy is bad. Imagination is good.
I think both philosopher and writer  
have to face a conflict between imagination 
and fantasy in themselves. But, of course,  
philosophy is an imaginative activity. And if one 
thinks of the great philosophers, they're very  
often picture-makers. They're people who produce 
enormous metaphors and pictures to explain things.
And the intrusion of personal, any kind of 
personal fantasy, is obviously out of place there.
I think there's an even greater conflict for 
the writer because creative imagination and  
personal fantasy are awfully 
close in relation to fiction.
I mean, the obvious sort of example is the case 
of the novel -- the bad novel -- which turns out  
to be simply a fantasy of how the hero, who is the 
writer, triumphs over all his enemies gets loved  
by the girls and so on and becomes rich and  
so forth. And this kind of fantasy is 
a menace to the creative imagination.
And in condemning art for being 
fantastic in this sense, one's  
very close to saying that it's untrue, that 
it's saying something which isn't true.
Now that conception of truth is 
different from what the philosopher is  
trying to get it, is it not?
Well, the philosopher is 
dealing with truth in a kind of  
positive sense, in that he's trying to establish.
He's trying to solve certain problems finally. 
Whether he ever does is another question. But  
I think there are certain philosophical 
problems, for instance about perception,  
which perhaps have been solved.
The writer has a whole lot of aims.
I mean, this is what, of course, makes 
art so interesting that it satisfies  
a great many human activities and instincts.
It produces happiness in the creator and in the  
client through doing a whole 
lot of things at the same time.
But one of the things it does -- and 
this I think is important and comes up in  
philosophical theories about art, 
which perhaps we might consider  
in a minute. There is, I think, a mimetic aspect.
What do you mean by mimetic?
That is that it is imitation of nature, in some 
sense, that literature is about the world. This  
might seem to be obvious. But of course it isn't 
obvious, in the sense that a lot of people would  
challenge this remark and say that literary art 
is not my mimetic. The same kind of question,  
of course, can be asked about painting. Music 
is an odd man out, I think, in this particular  
discussion. But I think that there are many 
parallels between painting and literature.
And these are sometimes illuminating.
We'll see something more clearly 
perhaps in the case of painting,  
which you then can transfer, as it 
were, to the case of literature.
You suggested just now that we move 
on to considering philosophical ideas  
about literature. And I think it 
would be very interesting to do that.
Which of the philosophical views of art that 
there have been would you pick out as being  
the most interesting to consider?  
Well, I find Plato's very interesting 
because it praises all those subjects.
Which one wants to clarify one's mind about?
What one wants to defend art?
I don't know. I think philosophers haven't written 
very well on art on the whole, partly because they  
very often put it in as a minor issue. They've got 
a general view of morals or metaphysics and so on,  
and they put in their view of art as a kind of 
piece that's got to be fitted into the picture.
And they don't, perhaps, consider it too seriously 
or too generously or not generously enough.
If I could jump in there. There is one 
philosopher whom I would exempt from  
that charge. I agree that, that is generally 
true. But I think Schopenhauer is an exception.  
I think he is genuinely deep on the subject of 
art. He, unlike nearly all other philosophers,  
he did regard art as being central to human life 
and had some very profound things to say about it.
This is perfectly true. Yes, I agree 
with you here. I mean, I think that Kant  
is very interesting about art. 
I think his distinction between  
the sublime and the beautiful -- beautiful 
is purely formal -- sublime is to do with  
kind of moral appeal to the emotions, 
which actually he didn't think of as  
being art at all. But that distinction, 
I think in itself, is very informative.
But on the whole,  
philosophers have tended to be polemical 
about art in a rather fruitless way. I mean,  
they've tended to produc e-- and, of course, any 
discussion about art tends endlessly to produce  
again and again a certain sort of "either or's" 
about art. Art's got to be one thing or the other.
I think this is the trouble with any philosophy 
of art, that it's exclusive. I mean, once you  
think that all art has got to be of a certain 
kind to fit in with your particular theory,  
then it follows from that, that everything 
that doesn't fit in with that theory is not.
I think fortunately artists don't pay too much 
attention to philosophers. They just carry on.
But I think philosophy can be 
damaging to art in a sense.  
I think the people it damages, of course, 
are students who long to have theories.
I mean, one sees this very often that they, 
young people, long to feel that art too can be  
explained or the aim of art can be explained.
I mean, these are very familiar 
sorts of fights of "either or's" that  
should art be for art's sake. Or 
should it be for society's sake.  
And, I mean, this is quite interesting too 
because again if one's instincts are to  
reject this choice -- which mine are -- than 
to say exactly why can be quite informative.
We've been talking up to this point about 
philosophy and literature or philosophical ideas  
about literature. Let's move on now to philosophy 
in literature. Let me say what I mean by that.
First of all, there have been some famous 
philosophers, or thinkers very like philosophers,  
like Voltaire, who have also been major novelists 
-- I mean, not only Voltaire but Rousseau,  
in our own time, Jean-Paul Sartre.
Or among simply famous 
novelists, there are some who've  
been obviously very profoundly influenced 
by certain rather philosophical ideas.
I mean, Tolstoy appends to his novel War 
and Peace a great long epilogue in which he  
explains that in this novel he's been trying 
to express a certain philosophy of history.
Or if you take another famous Russian 
novelist, Dostoevsky, he's often described by  
existentialists as the greatest of all 
existentialist writers because he's  
involved in certain kinds of problem that 
they themselves are also concerned with.
Or one can actually think of 
very large number of examples.
I mean, it's obvious that Proust 
in A la recherche du temps perdu is  
concerned, deeply concerned, 
with problems about time,  
which is also one of the traditional problems that 
philosophers have been concerned with and so on.  
What I would like to get at in the form 
of a question to you is: what sort of  
role do you think that philosophy can play in 
literature, in let us take novels for example?
Well, I think it had better keep out on the whole.
I think that people say these things about 
writers like Tolstoy and Dostoevsky in order  
to have something interesting to say about them.
I mean, obviously, writers are influenced 
by the climate of ideas of their time.
And they like to be educated people who know 
a little bit about philosophical change and so  
forth. But the amount of philosophy they succeed 
in expressing in the book is very small really.
I think as soon as philosophy gets into a novel,  
it ceases to be philosophy. 
It becomes something else.
It becomes a placing, obviously, of the writer and 
quite rightly. I mean, a pure novel of ideas -- I  
don't know that -- you know, it's very likely that 
it will -- the harder the writer works to present  
his ideas, the less good his work of art is likely 
to become. I think it's a very dangerous activity.
I think that, again, you see the great 19th 
century writers get away with quite a lot of  
idea play in their writings. 
But if you look at it,  
you couldn't possibly regard it 
as philosophy. It's idea play.
Well, I wonder if I agree 
with you. I mean, you say that  
people say this kind of thing about philosophers 
in order to have something interesting to say.
Well, no doubt there is a lot of 
pseudo-talk about novels of this kind.
But I did mention, for example, that 
Tolstoy himself says in War and Peace  
that this is one of the things 
he's trying to do. Or take a major  
English novel, like Laurence 
Sterne's Tristram Shandy.
I mean, not only was that 
directly influenced by Locke's  
theories about the association of 
ideas, but there's quite a bit about  
that in the novel and in terms which 
obviously refer to the novel itself.
In other words, Sterne was quite clearly 
doing something which he himself related  
to Locke's theory of the association of ideas.
So I think there are, in fact, a number 
of instances of great novels -- I mean,  
really outstanding novels -- where the  
use of philosophical ideas is part of the 
structure, is part of what it's about.
Yes. All right. I mean, perhaps it's just 
that I feel in myself such an absolute horror  
of putting theories as such into a book.
I mean, certain philosophical ideas might 
-- I mean, I'd perhaps put things about  
philosophy into my novels because 
I happen to know about philosophy.
I mean, if I knew about sailing ships,  
I'd put sailing ships in. If I knew 
about hospitals, I'd put hospitals in.
In a way, as a novelist, I'd rather not use 
sailing ships and hospitals about philosophy.
But I don't think that the 
writer necessarily knows.
In fact, you think it's just material.
Tolstoy says this. But I don't. Why should 
we attend to him? We read his books.
You know, don't trust the writer, trust the 
tale. One has to see what he's actually achieved.
And I can only think of one really good 
philosophical novel, which is Sartre's La Nausee,  
which I do admire very much, which might be 
said to be a philosophical novel, which is  
demonstrating something about contingency.
Interestingly, it's a philosophical subject, 
which is very close to the novelist's art.
I mean, there's kind of a good marriage there 
between the novelist's art and the subject.
Let's take that, shall we, 
Jean-Paul Sartre's La Nausee.
I mean, I agree with you. I think 
it's a magnificent book. But surely,  
it also articulates a philosophical theory.
In fact, my introduction to existentialism, 
so to speak, was reading that novel.
And I thought that, that novel actually  
expressed an existentialist view. And it's rather 
marvelous in the form of a work of fiction.
It may well be, as you say, unique. But the  
fact that it exists at all shows 
that it can be done, doesn't it?
Yes, you seem to be driving me to say 
something that I choose not to say.
I think there's a genuine difference 
of opinion between you and me,  
which we simply may not be able 
to resolve in this discussion.
But you, it seems to me, are trying to say that 
philosophy as such has no place in imaginative  
writing, except insofar as can simply be 
material like anything else can be material.
Whereas I think I want to say that there are 
really a number of exceptions to this and that  
some major novels do make use of  
philosophical ideas, not just as material in 
that sense but in ways which inform the book.
Yes.
I mean, this is perhaps just a matter of degree.
Obviously, there are things to do with the ideas 
current at the time, climate of ideas, and so on,  
which give a certain flavor to novels written 
at a certain time and incidentally which make it  
possible to write in one way at one time and 
impossible to write in that way at another time.
But I think when speaking ... 
I think existentialism may be  
something of a special case here, because 
it is a rather literary philosophy.  
It emphasizes all sorts of things 
to do the human predicament.
And Sartre's marvelous  
philosophical work L'Etre et le Neant is sort of 
full of pictures and conversations, as it were.
It's true of Hegel too actually that there's 
an awful lot of amusement and literary  
stuff in both Hegel and in Sartre.
And this is interesting because it's 
something to do with the rather dramatic  
form of the thought in the philosophy itself, that 
there's some kind of drama which can be exhibited.
There's some kind of feeling about the world,  
which belongs both to the 
philosophy and to the novel.
But one can see that it's not easy from looking at 
other works by Sartre and by Simone de Beauvoir,  
where as soon as the existentialist voice 
comes in, the work of art is rigidified.
I think it was just a lucky chance that 
he managed to pull it off in La Nausee.
I think given what you've now just said, 
I think perhaps we're not so for apart  
as I thought. Let me raise another kind 
of question with you, which, again,  
you as a practitioner of both philosophy and novel 
writing are almost uniquely well placed to answer.
One of the outstanding features of philosophy in 
the 20th century, especially in the Anglo-Saxon  
world, has been a completely new kind 
of concern with language, hasn't it,  
and self consciousness about language.
And this has resulted, among other things,  
in a sort of very refined and scrupulous 
use of words and concepts and so on.
I mean, in the case of somebody 
like yourself who trained as a  
philosopher and then became a novelist, do 
you think that has in any way entered into  
the way you write novels or 
affected the way you write novels?
No, I don't think so. I recognize what you mean 
about this particularness in language. Of course,  
the formalists are quite right to suggest that 
things have changed. This, of course, is something  
which every writer knows instinctively. You can 
no longer write like the 19th century novelists.
No.
And, of course, we're not so good. 
We're not anything like as good as  
the great 19th century novelists. 
But, also, we write differently.
And we would find it difficult to write as they 
did without an element of pastiche being involved.
Though I think some writers would actually 
regard this as a kind of challenge.
Why can't you write in the way?
I don't know. I mean, I can't 
exactly answer that question.
I mean, the obvious differences one has in 
mind here, of course, are things to do with the  
standpoint of the writer and the sense in which -- 
the way in which he inhabits the consciousness of  
his characters, his relation to 
his characters in this sense.
I think, that's something that one might say 
in a minute is that, of course, the author's  
relation to his characters reveals a great deal 
about the author's moral standpoint anyway.
But this is a technical point really about the 
author's relation to his characters. Unless he's  
playing some kind of game -- and, of course, the 
formalist would say he's always playing game.  
And the more games he plays, the better. But I 
would say, unless he's playing some kind of game,  
he will instinctively express himself in a 
way which is different, that he will inhabit  
the consciousness of his 
characters in an absolute sense.
He won't describe them from the outside. He'll 
describe them through consciousness. Or if he  
suddenly describes them from the outside, 
this will be an obvious literary device,  
which will hang up the reader for a moment.
Well, we are approaching the end 
of this discussion. And just now,  
a moment ago, you did say that you would 
like to take up the question of what the  
author reveals about his moral presuppositions.  
So shall we perhaps end with that? What is 
that you would like to say on that question?
Well, this would be part and parcel of my 
view of literature -- indeed my view of  
art -- which is that people sometimes say you 
must regard art and literature as being either  
the production of autonomous objects 
or a reflection of the world.
I mean, I think it's both. This seems to me 
to be obvious that it is both, almost always  
in some sense a reflection of the world -- perhaps 
not absolutely always, but almost always and quite  
properly so -- a reflection of the world. And 
it's also the production of a formal object.
I mean, one wouldn't call it a work of art unless 
it had certain formal properties and a certain  
kind of -- appropriate to the subject matter -- 
autonomy. Therefore, I would say art is mimetic.  
Language is referential, writers 
who themselves refer to the world.
And in pursuing this particular activity 
of producing literary work -- particularly,  
of course, fictions. And, particularly, of 
course, the novel is the most obvious case.  
One is inevitably involved 
in making moral judgments.
And this is something to do with the nature 
of language. If you tell somebody to describe  
their room or something, which one might 
think was a fairly neutral activity,  
this description will be 
crammed with value judgments.
And this is something which one can't avoid, 
not only in the choice of what they describe,  
which is important, but in the words 
that they use. Words are full of value.
And this is very obviously the case, of 
course, in the author's relation to his  
characters. And this comes back, partly, to this 
distinction between fantasy and imagination.  
If the relation is a fantasy relationship, the 
work is damaged both because the expansion -- the  
ability to exhibit the world in some way under 
a certain light -- is damaged and very often,  
of course, the values which the 
author expresses are improper ones.
And I think that the novelist may as well admit 
this, that he is going to be involved in making  
moral judgments. And his relationship to his 
characters is going to reveal his morality.
So there is a kind of moral challenge 
involved in art at a prior stage in the  
self discipline of the artist expelling fantasy 
and looking at things other than himself.
I think art is very much concerned 
with removing a veil of fantasy,  
which normally perhaps wraps one's 
head as one gazes at the world.
And looking at the world 
without this fantastic cover,  
the artist is concerned to see what is other 
than himself and in a sense to respect it  
and to have the self discipline to check the 
rashes of fantasy to which he may be subject.
So this work of imagination then creates a kind 
of space. And one does have a kind of feeling  
of imaginative space in great works of art, as 
if one was standing in a big hall or something.
At the prior level, he has to do this. And 
at a secondary level, when he's actually  
creating the work, he will find 
that he has to judge his characters.
And he has, in a way, to take sides. And 
this can be done, of course, in many ways.  
And, of course, ambiguity and 
mystifications come into this.
The author -- of course is enjoying himself all 
the time, as well as giving pleasure perhaps to  
the reader or aiming to entertain or even 
instruct the reader -- the author can play  
all sorts of mystifying games with the 
way in which he reveals his characters.
Henry James is full of such games for instance.  
But the revelation is there. 
And the judgment is there.
And, of course, what the author is 
doing all the time he's doing that is  
largely unconsciously revealing himself.
And this, of course, the unconscious self 
revelation is at its most obvious in bad art.
And I think to stick to this question 
about literature and the world,  
I think that the writer is engaged over a very, 
very large area of his personality in his writing.
And the writing and the work of art is judged 
against the background of the real world.
And by the real world, I mean 
the common sense real world.
Not some kind of [INAUDIBLE] real world, 
which would need special implements to  
discover, but the world which we take to be 
real in ordinary life and in common sense.
Does this mean that imaginative writing 
must -- although it's imaginative -- be  
rooted in some kind of respect for things as 
they are and acceptance of things as they are?
Yes, I think so.
I think that this -- I mean, one can play 
with this idea in relation to painting.
And as I said very often, painting 
shows you something about literature.
Someone might say, well, obviously 
painting isn't always mimetic.
What about abstract painting?
Well, yes, certainly.
But I think it's very difficult to judge 
abstract painting actually. And I think  
a lot of funny stuff is appearing now, 
which perhaps might be very difficult  
to justify in any clear, critical vocabulary.
But one works at a critical 
vocabulary, while one works at an art.
The critic and the artist have a kind of natural 
organic relationship. But the abstract painting  
lives in a world which isn't abstract painting. 
And it's connected with that world in deep ways.  
And, obviously, one can distinguish between a good 
abstract painting and a bad abstract painting.
And this is something to do with the real 
world. Hard to explain perhaps, but it's there.
And it's very much more obvious and 
simple, of course, in the case of a  
novel, where ideas of plausibility and 
implausibility and fantasy and so on --
Do you think that this acceptance of reality 
implies something conservative with a small  
c? I'm not talking any in terms that relate 
at all to party politics but to temperament.
Do you think this acceptance of reality  
as it is implies some kind of conservatism 
of outlook on the part of an artist?
It's an interesting point, yes. I'm 
not sure. I meant to try to illustrate  
perhaps more clearly just to myself this 
thing about common sense being involved.
There are philosophical ideas, which sometimes 
get picked up by writers. For instance, the  
whole collection of ideas connected with the  
self not being a kind of unitary object, 
the self being fragmentary and so on.
And this is an idea which a lot of writers of 
course play with. But the force of their play,  
takes place in a world where we 
normally take the self to be continuous.
And we would hold a very odd 
belief about various subjects,  
such as moral responsibility, if we 
thought that the self was discontinuous.
And this is just to say again what I said 
about the abstract painting lives in the world  
of where we are interested in real objects and 
colors as part of objects and so on. I don't know.
I mean, this would be an interesting sort 
of theory to say that artists are always  
conservatives because they have a tolerant -- 
I think there's a kind of tolerance involved.
I don't know about conservative exactly, 
but I think that a great artist has got a  
kind of tolerance because he can see 
an awful lot of what's really there.
I mean, Shakespeare can see an 
enormous amount. I think there's  
a kind of wrath of tolerance which comes out 
of Shakespeare because he can see so much.
He can see how awfully different, different 
people are and what makes them different  
and how many different ways there 
are of thinking about the world.
And I think this is a kind of virtue. And I think 
it's this virtue of tolerance which a lot of  
dictatorial art, as it were, 
is deliberately excluding.
Well, I think wherever we finish this discussion,  
Iris Murdoch, it would have a 
very sort of unfinished feel.
But that's in the nature of the subject.
And I think we are going to have to stop now.
Thank you very much indeed.
